context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class about aid and development. I’m kind of stuck on how to organize my response to the prompt, especially balancing Easterly vs. Sachs without sounding too one-sided.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Before we think about structure, let's dig into the ideas themselves. The prompt sets up a tension between Easterly and Sachs. In your own words, what's the core disagreement between them on aid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for organizational help before they've even started thinking through the substance. Rather than helping them structure their paper (which would be doing their work for them), I should redirect them to first engage with the actual intellectual content of the prompt. A good Socratic opening question will help them surface their own thinking and reveal where they actually stand on the issues. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead with their own ideas first.

exchanges.student: I think Sachs is basically saying aid can help countries escape poverty if it’s big enough and coordinated, and Easterly thinks that kind of top-down aid is too optimistic and usually doesn’t work the way planners think. I’m not totally sure if that’s too simplified, but it seems like one is more pro-aid and the other is more skeptical about large-scale aid-led development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point. Now push it a little further — the prompt isn't just asking you to describe their positions, it's asking you to evaluate them. Do you think Easterly's skepticism throws the baby out with the bathwater, or does he have a point that Sachs misses? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable grasp of the Easterly/Sachs divide. It's a fair summary, not too far off. I should affirm the core of it without over-praising, then push them to go deeper with a Socratic question that moves toward the prompt's actual question: is Easterly 'overly dismissive' and is Sachs 'unrealistic'? This gets them thinking critically rather than just summarizing positions.

exchanges.student: My instinct is that Easterly has a real point about aid failure, incentives, and outside actors acting like they can engineer development, but he might go too far if that turns into “aid mostly doesn’t help.” I don’t really buy Sachs either when it sounds like enough money plus expert planning will fix structural problems. So I guess I’m landing in the middle, but I’m not sure how to make that sound sharper instead of just vague.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — but 'somewhere in the middle' only gets sharp when you can name the condition that makes the difference. The prompt actually gives you a useful tool here: what do you think changes when you distinguish between short-term and long-term aid? Does that distinction help you sharpen your position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a substantive position forming - they're not fully buying either side. The risk is that 'middle ground' becomes wishy-washy. I should push them to sharpen their critique by asking them to think about what specific distinction or condition would make aid work or not work. The prompt itself hints at this with the short-term vs. long-term aid distinction, which is a natural way to sharpen their middle position without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that helps a lot. Short-term aid seems easier for me to defend morally and practically, like disaster relief or emergency health support, while long-term aid is where the bigger problems show up because it can create dependency or ignore politics and institutions. I’m still fuzzy though on whether long-term aid is bad in itself, or just bad when it’s too top-down.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharper question than you might realize. So if the problem isn't long-term aid itself but something about how it's designed or delivered — what would 'good' long-term aid actually look like, in your view? What conditions would need to be in place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has identified a key nuance - the problem might not be long-term aid per se, but how it's delivered. This is a good insight worth pushing further. Rather than validating or expanding on it myself, I should ask them to think about what conditions or characteristics would make long-term aid work better, which will help them develop a sharper argument rather than just a critique.

exchanges.student: I think good long-term aid would have to support local institutions instead of replacing them, and it probably needs to be accountable to people in the country receiving it, not just donors. Maybe it should also be more modest about what it can do, like building capacity or public health systems instead of trying to impose a whole development model. I’m a little unsure because “capacity building” sounds nice but also kind of vague.
exchanges.tutor: Your skepticism about 'capacity building' is healthy — it's worth keeping that critical eye. Now, the prompt raises something you haven't touched yet: what happens to aid when it gets tied to security agendas like counter-insurgency? How does that sit with your argument about local accountability and modest goals?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a solid substantive argument. Their hesitation about 'capacity building' being vague is actually a sign of good critical thinking. I should affirm that instinct and push them to think about the security/counter-insurgency angle the prompt raises, which they haven't touched yet and which connects naturally to their concern about aid being used for external agendas rather than local accountability. This moves the conversation forward rather than dwelling on capacity building.

exchanges.student: That seems like it makes the whole thing messier, because then aid is not mainly about development anymore — it’s also serving donor-state security interests. That probably weakens local legitimacy and can distort where money goes, since it might follow strategic priorities instead of actual need. So I guess that supports a more critical view of aid, even if I still think some aid is necessary.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're building a consistent thread. There's one piece of the prompt you haven't tackled yet: is there a moral case for aid, and what is it? Given everything you've said, how would you make that argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is connecting ideas well and building a coherent argument. They've now addressed most of the prompt's components. The one piece they haven't engaged with yet is the moral case for aid, which the prompt explicitly asks about. This is a natural next step and will round out their reflection. I'll push them there directly.

exchanges.student: I think the moral case is that if people have the resources to prevent extreme suffering, they should help, especially when global inequality is tied to historical and political structures, not just bad luck. But I don’t think the moral case automatically proves that any form of aid is good, which is where I want to connect ethics to effectiveness and accountability. Is that too abstract?
exchanges.tutor: Not too abstract at all — linking the moral case to accountability is actually one of the stronger moves you can make. Last piece: the prompt asks how you judge the effectiveness of aid. Given everything you've argued, what criteria would you use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a strong moral argument that connects well to their earlier points about accountability and effectiveness. This isn't too abstract — it's actually a sophisticated move to link the normative and empirical dimensions. I should affirm this and push them to also address the prompt's last question about judging effectiveness, which they haven't directly answered yet. This will complete their substantive thinking before they write.

exchanges.student: I’d judge it by whether it reduces suffering in a real, measurable way, but also whether it strengthens or undermines local institutions and political legitimacy over time. I think I’d also ask who defines success, because if only donors are deciding that, the evaluation feels biased. I’m close to seeing my argument now, but I could use help turning this into a clear thesis.
exchanges.tutor: You have all the ingredients — I won't formulate the thesis for you, but you can. Look back at what you've argued: your position on Easterly vs. Sachs, the short/long-term distinction, local accountability, the security agenda problem, the moral case, and your effectiveness criteria. Try writing one or two sentences that capture your core argument. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them formulate their thesis, which would cross the line into doing their writing for them. I should firmly but supportively decline and instead ask them to try writing a thesis sentence themselves based on everything we've discussed. They have all the ingredients - they just need to put it together. This is a critical moment where I need to hold the boundary clearly.

exchanges.student: Maybe: aid is necessary in a moral sense and often essential in the short term, but its long-term developmental value depends on whether it supports locally legitimate institutions rather than donor-driven agendas. Easterly is right to criticize top-down, overconfident aid, but he is too dismissive of aid’s real humanitarian and institutional benefits, while Sachs is too optimistic about what external funding can achieve when politics and power are ignored.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong start — the first sentence is doing real argumentative work. The second sentence, though, reads more like a description of the debate than your own claim. Can you fold your evaluation of Easterly and Sachs into your argument, rather than presenting it as a separate point about them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good thesis that captures the key tensions in the prompt and takes a clear position. I should affirm its strengths without grading it, but also push them to notice one potential weakness: the second sentence reads more like a summary of Easterly and Sachs than an extension of the argument. I want them to see if they can tighten that into their own claim rather than a description of the debate. This is feedback-oriented and moves toward writing, which is the natural next step given we've covered all the main ideas.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I see that. Maybe I should make them examples of the larger problem: one side underestimates aid because it focuses on failure, and the other overestimates it because it treats development as too technocratic. I’m not sure how to phrase that cleanly in one sentence though.
exchanges.tutor: You understand what you want to say — now just try writing it. Don't aim for perfect; aim for a sentence that captures that idea. You can always revise. And at this point, you have enough to start drafting the full piece. What's stopping you from just getting words on the page?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea conceptually - they want to subordinate the Easterly/Sachs debate to their larger argument. They're asking for help phrasing it, which would cross into writing for them. I should push them to just try writing it themselves - they've been building toward this and have enough to attempt it. Also, we're at 9 exchanges and have covered all the main ideas thoroughly. It's time to push them toward drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea — the Easterly/Sachs distinction, the short/long-term framework, local accountability, the security agenda complication, the moral case, and effectiveness criteria — was drawn out from the student rather than supplied by the tutor. The tutor consistently asked the student to push further, name conditions, and generate their own thesis, refusing to formulate it even when directly asked. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building each turn on the student's prior response. Responses were concise and targeted. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave a final answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student reasoned toward it — all idea generation was student-led, with the tutor only prompting deeper thinking. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'that's a sharper question than you might realize') and used diagnostic questions before redirecting. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably pointing out that the second thesis sentence 'reads more like a description of the debate than your own claim' and directing the student to fold evaluation into argument. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misalignment, and no off-topic drift. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
